title: Is olive oil making us fat?

chunks.title: Introduction to Echidnas
chunks.summary: The speaker introduces echidnas, unique mammals that lay eggs and are closely related to platypuses. They have distinctive feeding habits and hibernation patterns.

chunks.title: Echidna Diet and Fat Composition
chunks.summary: The discussion shifts to the dietary habits of echidnas, highlighting their consumption of ants rich in unsaturated fats. The speaker explains the changes in fat composition as echidnas approach hibernation.

chunks.title: Comparative Analysis of Ant Fat Composition
chunks.summary: The video examines studies comparing fatty acid compositions of ants at different latitudes, noting differences between tropical and non-tropical species. The relationship between geographical location and fat types in ants is analyzed.
chunks.transcript: a percentage the amount of monounsaturated fat goes down um and they come out of and when they come out of hibernation they're a lot more saturated now one of the reasons that's interesting is that if you look at and I talked about this in the same video if you look at ants at the equator um there was this paper in out of Brazil and they looked at like 30 different tropical ant species and those ants are very high in steric acid they have a ratio of some of them have more steric acid than they had of oleic acid which is very rare and certainly and Foods grown far from the equator you almost never find that OIC acid is the the main fat in so many of the different like any animal fats um but these Brazilian ants and like I say 30 different species of ant it's not like they found this one species they looked at every ant they could find in Brazil and they all have this very high ratio of saturated fat to monounsaturated fat and and ants don't have a lot of polyunsaturated fat and then they looked in Germany and they looked at maybe seven different ant species in Germany I don't think there are as many species of ant in Germany as there are in Brazil um and all of those German ants had about 20 times or more as much oleic acid as they had of steric acid um and so as you move uh farther from the equ higher in Latitude whether you go north or south um what happens is the ratio of steric acid to OIC acid changes in the food that the first mammals would have eaten and so mamal the earliest mammals uh mostly ate insects they were small

chunks.title: Impact of Dietary Fats on Metabolism
chunks.summary: The speaker discusses research indicating how various fats impact metabolic rates in mice. A specific focus is placed on the effects of saturated versus unsaturated fats on energy expenditure.
chunks.transcript: like ants um and so when you move from the equator you're you know and and this is sort of paralleled right in the in the plant kingdom because you think about cocoa butter right cocoa butter is a tropical plant very saturated lots of steric acid and then you know olive oil is grown more in the north and that has a lot of oleic acid and not much steric acid just like the ants right and and this is of course um partially due to weather reasons uh when it's cold obviously saturated fat gets very hard and it's probably hard for a um you know if an olive had the same fat composition as a cocoa bean it would be hard to kind of mobilize those fats and for the Olive to run its metabolism and the olive seed to grow on a cold day you know in a in a even even somewhere like Italy which is mostly uh reasonably warm but they still have cold days in the winter and so you know so as you get away from the equator you see a lot more monounsaturated fat and the saturated fat in the Aida Peaks right before it goes into hibernation and so you you put those facts together and then there's a great series of paper of papers from James nambi and in the lab what he shows is if you remove that Gene the one that converts the saturated fats to monounsaturated fats in a mouse uh the mice can't get fat um so so that so these are called s this gene is called scd1 and so if you take an scd1 deficient mice or mouse or a scd1 knockout M Mouse they they have super high metabolic rates their metabolic rates are like 40% higher than um than a normal Mouse uh and it's only because they can't make monounsaturated that and so then also in that same John Speakman paper that we already mentioned he showed that um in mice they they fed him all these different oils at like 40% of calories and he showed that the ones fed the most saturated fat had the highest metabolic rates and as they fed them more and more and more unsaturated fats the metabolic rates in these mice

chunks.title: Oleic Acid and Lipogenesis
chunks.summary: This segment addresses the role of oleic acid in stimulating lipogenesis and its activation of related transcription factors. Evidence supports the notion that different oils affect lipid metabolism in animals.
chunks.transcript: series of papers what one of the really interesting ones he wrote was we have something called shrep which is a they call it the the master lipogenic transcription Factor right and so this is a this is a transcription factor that that turns on your genes involved in denovo lipogenesis which means making fat right so if you eat carbohydrate or if you eat protein if you have upregulated lipogenic enzymes you'll turn more of that into fat and what nmi found was that um the oleic acid binds to that the master uh lipogenic transcription factor shrep and activates it and so the transcription factor that controls lipogenesis is actually activated by monounsaturated fats and and so what you see so there's a positive feedback loop there where uh the mammal is is as it moves away from the equator it's eating and that are getting higher and higher in monounsaturated fat and that monounsaturated fat is hitting all kinds of signals one it's activating PPA Alpha um when you consume it and PPA Alpha is a very interesting um that's another highlevel transcription Factor that's kind of monitoring the fats that you eat and it's causing your metabolism to behave accordingly and then um these monounsaturated fats are actually activating these PR lipogenic uh enzymes and if you look in feeding studies where they take a rat or a pig and you feed them five different kinds of oil um you know there's one in uh rats that I'm sure that I've I've shared with you but there's one where they fed them beef Tallow or they fed them olive oil or they fed them I believe it was holic uh or sorry High linolic sunflower oil um the ones the olive oil had the highest expression of lipogenic enzymes in their liver by a lot and then you see the same thing happens in pigs and so you know you have like uh so we have on a lot of different levels it appears that o uh oleic acid the monounsaturated fat can be a real

chunks.title: Cultural Perspectives on Fat Consumption
chunks.summary: The conversation transitions to cultural attitudes towards fats, particularly olive oil. The speaker contrasts obesity rates among populations consuming different types of fats and discusses the implications of dietary habits.
chunks.transcript: metabolic rate um and just to throw one other thing out there there's another nambi paper where they showed that the oleic acid prevents the breakdown of circulating cannaboids um and so and so the cannaboids are endogenous molecules that we make that hit our cannaboid receptors and kind of can give you the munchies right if you have two many of these cannaboids and and the the OIC acid is preventing the breakdown of the cannaboids and so you see like on eight different levels the oleic acid is part of this positive feedback back Loop of you know animals in the north kind of fattening for winter um and the other thing and just one more fact on that um idea the ants in Germany that have a lot more OIC acid also have a lot higher uh body fat percentage so as the ants move away from the equator they have more OIC acid and they're fatter um and so you know you see so you see what to me looks like this kind of over overwhelming evidence that oleic acid is really part of the problem um and this is of course shocking to people because we're always being told that olive oil is great um and that people from Italy are lean and healthy um and there's a funny there's a funny story about that so uh you know of course there's different ways that we have of assessing a population and how many people are obese and one of the ways is you just send people like a like a a flyer in the mail and they write down their their height and their weight and they send it back in and then we do other studies where they take a representative population sample and you have to go into the clinic and get measured by you know a nurse or a health care professional and interestingly the Italians do really well in terms of obesity rates on the first kind of study where you just ask them what their weight is but if you weigh them you find out that that they're actually uh significantly more

chunks.title: Obesity Studies and Dietary Signals
chunks.summary: The video evaluates how dietary choices signal the body regarding fat storage and metabolism. The speaker emphasizes the importance of understanding nutrient ratios in combating obesity.

chunks.title: Regional Differences in Health Outcomes
chunks.summary: The final segment highlights geographical disparities in obesity and health outcomes within Italy and Spain. The speaker advocates for a nuanced understanding of how dietary patterns impact human health.
